title: Travel Adventures
warmup: What is the most interesting place you have visited?

vocabulary.example: We have a packed itinerary for our trip to Tokyo.
vocabulary.question: Do you prefer a strict itinerary or spontaneous travel?

vocabulary.example: Her wanderlust took her to five different continents in one year.
vocabulary.question: How do you satisfy your wanderlust when you can't travel?

idioms.example: We hit the road early in the morning
idioms.question: When do you usually hit the road?

idioms.meaning: In a place where few people go
idioms.example: We found a tiny restaurant off the beaten path.
idioms.question: Do you like visiting famous landmarks or going off the beaten path?

grammar.title: Present Perfect for Experiences
grammar.explanation: Use 'have/has + past participle' to talk about things you have done in your life without a specific time.
grammar.question: Have you ever traveled alone?

activities: Describe your best trip and why it was memorable.
Is traveling alone better than with friends? Debate with your partner.
Scenario: Your flight is cancelled and the next one is in 24 hours. What do you do?
Roleplay: A travel agent trying to sell a trip to a very picky customer.
Do you think tourism is generally good or bad for local cultures?
wrapup: Where would you travel if money was unlimited?
teacherTips: Encourage follow-up questions about specific travel details.
Let students compare their favorite types of accommodation.

title: Eating Out
warmup: What is your favorite food to eat at a restaurant?

vocabulary.definition: A style or method of cooking
vocabulary.example: I love Italian cuisine, especially pasta.
vocabulary.question: Which international cuisine is your favorite?
What is the most delicious thing you can cook?

vocabulary.definition: One of the foods that are used to make a dish
vocabulary.example: Fresh ingredients are the secret to a good salad.
vocabulary.question: What are the three main ingredients in your favorite dish?

idioms.meaning: Something that is very easy to do
idioms.example: The exam was a piece of cake.
idioms.question: What is a skill that was a piece of cake for you to learn?

idioms.example: Don't spill the beans about the surprise party!
idioms.question: Is it hard for you to keep a secret? Have you ever accidentally spilled the beans?

grammar.title: Countable and Uncountable Nouns
grammar.explanation: Use 'a/an' or numbers for countable nouns. Use 'some/any' for uncountable nouns.
grammar.example: I have an apple. I have some water.
grammar.question: What do you usually have in your fridge? Mention 3 countable and 3 uncountable items.

activities: Talk about the last time you went to a restaurant.
Do you prefer home-cooked meals or fast food? Why?
Scenario: You received the wrong order at a restaurant. How do you tell the waiter?
Roleplay: Customer and a waiter who is being very slow.
If you could only eat one food for the rest of your life, what would it be?
wrapup: What is the most unusual thing you have ever eaten?
teacherTips: Use photos of food to prompt vocabulary.
Ask students to describe tastes (sweet, salty, spicy).

title: The AI Revolution
warmup: How often do you use Artificial Intelligence in your daily life?

vocabulary.example: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern society.
vocabulary.question: What technology do you think will be ubiquitous in 10 years?

vocabulary.definition: A new method, idea, or product
vocabulary.example: Technological innovation is driving economic growth.
vocabulary.question: What is the most important innovation of the 21st century?

vocabulary.definition: To make a process run by machines rather than people
vocabulary.example: Many factories are trying to automate their production lines.
vocabulary.question: Which parts of your job or studies would you like to automate?

idioms.meaning: Thinking in a similar way to someone else
idioms.example: We're on the same wavelength about the project.
idioms.question: Who is someone you are always on the same wavelength with?

idioms.meaning: The most advanced or latest stage of development
idioms.example: This company is at the cutting edge of medical research.
idioms.question: What is the most cutting-edge device you own?

grammar.title: Passive Voice for Processes
grammar.explanation: Use 'be + past participle' to focus on the action or the object rather than the subject.
grammar.example: Data is processed by AI algorithms.
grammar.question: How are smartphones manufactured? Try to use the passive voice.

activities: Discuss: Will AI eventually take over most human jobs?
Debate: Is social media making us more or less connected?
Scenario: You wake up and all technology in the world has stopped working. What is your first day like?
Roleplay: An elderly person asking a teenager to explain how to use a new app.
What are the ethical concerns regarding facial recognition technology?
wrapup: If you could invent one piece of technology to solve a world problem, what would it be?
teacherTips: Encourage use of complex sentence structures.
Help students with formal technological terminology.

title: Fun After School
warmup: What do you like to do after you finish your homework?

vocabulary.definition: A group of things that are the same type
vocabulary.example: I have a big collection of toy cars.
vocabulary.question: Do you have a collection of anything?

vocabulary.definition: An activity done for pleasure in one's free time
vocabulary.question: What is your favorite hobby?

vocabulary.definition: Doing something again and again to get better
vocabulary.example: I practice the piano every day.
vocabulary.question: What do you need to practice to get better at?

idioms.meaning: A person who watches a lot of TV and does not exercise
idioms.example: Don't be a couch potato! Let's go outside.
idioms.question: Is it okay to be a couch potato sometimes? When?

idioms.meaning: To have a very fun time
idioms.example: We had a blast at the birthday party.
idioms.question: When was the last time you had a blast?

grammar.title: Like / Enjoy + -ing
grammar.explanation: Use the '-ing' form of a verb after 'like' or 'enjoy'.
grammar.example: I like swimming. I enjoy playing games.
grammar.question: What are three things you like doing on the weekend?

activities: Show and tell: Describe your favorite toy or game.
What is something new you want to try this year?
Scenario: You want to start a club at school. What is the club and what do you do?
Roleplay: One student is a teacher and the other is a student. The teacher asks about the student's weekend.
Draw your favorite hobby and explain it to the class.
wrapup: If you could spend the whole day doing only one thing, what would it be?
teacherTips: Keep the energy high and use simple words.
Use gestures and drawings to help explain meanings.
